questions.question:
How many distinct BSTs can be constructed with 3 distinct keys?

A. 4
B. 5
C. 6
D. 9
questions.to_find: The number of distinct Binary Search Trees that can be formed using 3 distinct keys.

understand.plain: A Binary Search Tree (BST) is a binary tree where every node's left subtree contains only smaller keys and right subtree contains only larger keys. For 3 distinct keys, we need to count how many structurally different BST shapes are possible (since the relative ordering is fixed by BST property, each shape corresponds to a unique BST). The count of distinct BSTs with n keys is given by the nth Catalan number.

keywords.explain: A binary tree where for every node, all keys in the left subtree are smaller and all keys in the right subtree are larger.
keywords.example: Keys {1,2,3}: if 2 is root, 1 goes left and 3 goes right.

keywords.explain: The nth Catalan number C(n) = C(2n,n)/(n+1) counts the number of distinct BST structures with n keys.
keywords.example: C(3) = C(6,3)/4 = 20/4 = 5, so 5 distinct BSTs with 3 keys.

understand.visual_alt: Five distinct BST structures with 3 keys: right-leaning chain, root=1 with right subtree, balanced tree with root=2, root=3 with left subtree, and left-leaning chain.

given.aim: Count the number of distinct BSTs that can be formed with 3 distinct keys.

terms.meaning: Number of distinct keys to be inserted into the BST.
terms.example: Keys could be {1, 2, 3} or any set of 3 distinct values.
terms.connects: We enumerate all BSTs by choosing each key as root and recursively counting left and right subtrees.

terms.meaning: Total number of distinct BST structures with n keys.
terms.connects: T(n) = sum_{i=1}^{n} T(i-1) * T(n-i), which equals the nth Catalan number.

terms.meaning: C(n) = C(2n,n)/(n+1). The number of structurally distinct BSTs with n nodes.
terms.connects: Directly gives the answer: 5 distinct BSTs with 3 keys.

given.plan: Use the recurrence T(n) = sum_{i=1}^{n} T(i-1)*T(n-i). Compute T(0), T(1), T(2) first, then T(3). Alternatively, use the Catalan number formula C(2n,n)/(n+1) with n=3.

steps.title: Establish base cases
steps.apply: An empty tree (0 nodes) counts as 1 structure. A single-node tree also has exactly 1 structure.
steps.note: T(0)=1 is critical: it represents the empty left or right subtree when a key has no smaller/larger companions.

steps.title: Compute T(2)
steps.apply:
Root = key1: left subtree has 0 keys (T(0)=1), right has 1 key (T(1)=1) -> 1*1=1.
Root = key2: left subtree has 1 key (T(1)=1), right has 0 keys (T(0)=1) -> 1*1=1.
T(2) = 1+1 = 2.
steps.note: Two shapes: one a right-leaning chain, one a left-leaning chain.

steps.title: Compute T(3)
steps.apply:
Root = smallest key: T(0)*T(2) = 1*2 = 2 (two right-subtree shapes).
Root = middle key: T(1)*T(1) = 1*1 = 1 (balanced tree).
Root = largest key: T(2)*T(0) = 2*1 = 2 (two left-subtree shapes).
T(3) = 2+1+2 = 5.
steps.note: This matches the 3rd Catalan number: C(6,3)/4 = 20/4 = 5.

solution.result: 5 distinct BSTs can be constructed with 3 distinct keys. Answer: B. 5

questions.formula_note: Catalan number formula: number of distinct BSTs with n keys = C(2n,n)/(n+1). For n=3: C(6,3)/4 = 5.

questions.question:
State whether the following statement is TRUE or FALSE:

It is possible to construct a binary tree uniquely whose pre-order and post-order traversals are given.

A. TRUE
B. FALSE
questions.to_find: Whether a binary tree can be uniquely constructed given only its pre-order and post-order traversals.

understand.plain: Pre-order visits root, then left subtree, then right subtree. Post-order visits left subtree, then right subtree, then root. Given both, we still cannot always uniquely reconstruct the tree — specifically, if a node has only one child, we cannot determine whether that child is a left child or a right child. So the tree is NOT always uniquely determined.

keywords.explain: Tree traversal order: Root -> Left subtree -> Right subtree.
keywords.example: For tree with root A having only right child B: pre-order = A B.

keywords.explain: Tree traversal order: Left subtree -> Right subtree -> Root.
keywords.example: For same tree: post-order = B A.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='400' height='200' viewBox='0 0 400 200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='400' height='200' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='200' y='28' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>Two different trees, same pre- and post-order</text><g transform='translate(60,50)'><circle cx='40' cy='24' r='16' class='hi'/><text x='40' y='29' class='tag ink' text-anchor='middle'>A</text><circle cx='16' cy='84' r='16' class='ok'/><text x='16' y='89' class='tag' fill='white' text-anchor='middle'>B</text><line x1='30' y1='38' x2='20' y2='68' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='40' y='140' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>B is LEFT child of A</text><text x='40' y='160' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>Pre: A B  Post: B A</text></g><text x='200' y='110' class='big ink' text-anchor='middle'>vs</text><g transform='translate(240,50)'><circle cx='40' cy='24' r='16' class='hi'/><text x='40' y='29' class='tag ink' text-anchor='middle'>A</text><circle cx='64' cy='84' r='16' class='no'/><text x='64' y='89' class='tag' fill='white' text-anchor='middle'>B</text><line x1='50' y1='38' x2='60' y2='68' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='60' y='140' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>B is RIGHT child of A</text><text x='60' y='160' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>Pre: A B  Post: B A</text></g></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two trees both with root A and one child B: in one tree B is the left child, in the other B is the right child. Both produce identical pre-order (A B) and post-order (B A) sequences, proving uniqueness is impossible.

given.aim: Determine if pre-order + post-order traversals uniquely identify a binary tree.

terms.meaning: The sequence of nodes visited in Root-Left-Right order.
terms.example: Pre-order of a tree with root A and left child B: A, B.
terms.connects: Gives us root first; helps identify subtree roots but doesn't fix left vs right when a node has one child.

terms.meaning: The sequence of nodes visited in Left-Right-Root order.
terms.example: Post-order of same tree: B, A.
terms.connects: Gives us root last; combined with pre-order still leaves ambiguity for one-child nodes.

terms.meaning: When a node has exactly one child, pre+post order cannot tell us if it is the left or right child.
terms.example: A tree with root 1 and child 2: if 2 is left child, pre=1,2 post=2,1; if 2 is right child, pre=1,2 post=2,1. Both give the same traversals.
terms.connects: This is the counterexample that makes the statement FALSE.

given.plan: Construct a counterexample: a tree where a node has only one child. Show that the same pre-order and post-order sequences arise from two different trees (one with left child, one with right child).

steps.title: Counterexample: tree with one child
steps.apply:
Consider tree T1: root = A, left child = B (no right child).
Pre-order of T1: A, B.
Post-order of T1: B, A.

Consider tree T2: root = A, right child = B (no left child).
Pre-order of T2: A, B.
Post-order of T2: B, A.

Both T1 and T2 produce the same pre-order and post-order sequences, yet they are different trees.
steps.note: The problem arises whenever any node has exactly one child. In-order traversal is needed along with either pre-order or post-order to reconstruct a binary tree uniquely.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.apply: Since we found two distinct binary trees with the same pre-order and post-order, it is NOT always possible to construct a binary tree uniquely from pre-order and post-order alone. The statement is FALSE.
steps.note: Note: For a FULL binary tree (every node has 0 or 2 children, none has exactly 1 child), pre-order + post-order IS sufficient for unique reconstruction. But in general the statement is FALSE.

solution.result: FALSE. The statement is false. Pre-order and post-order traversals together do not uniquely determine a binary tree. Answer: B. FALSE

questions.formula_note: For unique binary tree reconstruction: pre-order + in-order OR post-order + in-order is sufficient. Pre-order + post-order is NOT sufficient in general (ambiguity for single-child nodes).

questions.question:
State whether the following statement is TRUE or FALSE:

If the number of leaves in a tree is not a power of 2, then the tree is not a binary tree.

A. TRUE
B. FALSE
questions.to_find: Whether a binary tree must have a number of leaves that is a power of 2.

understand.plain: A binary tree is a tree where each node has at most 2 children (left and/or right). The number of leaves (nodes with no children) in a binary tree can be any positive integer — it does not have to be a power of 2. For example, a binary tree can easily have 3 leaves. So the statement is FALSE.

keywords.explain: A tree in which each node has at most 2 children, called the left child and the right child.
keywords.example: A root with two children, each of whom has one child: 3 leaf nodes.

keywords.explain: A node with no children (both left and right child pointers are null).
keywords.example: In a tree of 3 nodes (root and two children), the two children are leaf nodes.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='320' height='200' viewBox='0 0 320 200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='320' height='200' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='160' y='28' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>Binary tree with 3 leaves (not power of 2)</text><circle cx='160' cy='60' r='18' class='hi'/><text x='160' y='65' class='tag ink' text-anchor='middle'>A</text><circle cx='100' cy='110' r='18' class='hi'/><text x='100' y='115' class='tag ink' text-anchor='middle'>B</text><circle cx='220' cy='110' r='18' class='hi'/><text x='220' y='115' class='tag ink' text-anchor='middle'>C</text><circle cx='70' cy='160' r='18' class='ok'/><text x='70' y='165' class='tag' fill='white' text-anchor='middle'>D</text><circle cx='130' cy='160' r='18' class='ok'/><text x='130' y='165' class='tag' fill='white' text-anchor='middle'>E</text><circle cx='220' cy='160' r='18' class='ok'/><text x='220' y='165' class='tag' fill='white' text-anchor='middle'>F</text><line x1='148' y1='74' x2='112' y2='96' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='172' y1='74' x2='208' y2='96' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='92' y1='124' x2='78' y2='146' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='108' y1='124' x2='122' y2='146' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='220' y1='128' x2='220' y2='142' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='160' y='195' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>Leaves: D, E, F = 3 leaves (3 is not a power of 2)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A binary tree with root A, children B and C. B has children D and E (both leaves). C has one child F (a leaf). Total 3 leaves — a valid binary tree despite 3 not being a power of 2.

given.aim: Determine whether a binary tree must have a number of leaves that is a power of 2.

terms.meaning: A tree where each node has 0, 1, or 2 children.
terms.example: A root with two children, and one of them has one more child, is a valid binary tree.
terms.connects: This definition places NO restriction on the number of leaves.

terms.meaning: Count of nodes with no children in the tree.
terms.example: For a root with left child only: 1 leaf. For a root with 2 children: 2 leaves.
terms.connects: Can be any positive integer; does not have to be a power of 2.

terms.meaning: Numbers like 1, 2, 4, 8, 16, ... = 2^k for some non-negative integer k.
terms.connects: The claim is these are impossible as leaf counts in a binary tree, which is false.

given.plan: Construct a simple counterexample: a binary tree with exactly 3 leaves (3 is not a power of 2) to disprove the statement.

steps.title: Construct a counterexample
steps.apply:
Build the following binary tree:
- Root A with left child B and right child C.
- B has left child D and right child E (both are leaves).
- C has only one child F (a leaf).

Leaf count = {D, E, F} = 3, which is NOT a power of 2.
Yet this is clearly a valid binary tree (each node has at most 2 children).
steps.note: Any binary tree where some internal node has exactly one child can produce a leaf count that is not a power of 2.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.apply: Since a valid binary tree with 3 leaves exists, the claim that 'if the number of leaves is not a power of 2, the tree is not a binary tree' is FALSE. There is no such restriction in the definition of binary trees.
steps.note: The property that leaf count = 2^k holds only for PERFECT binary trees (all leaves at the same level, all internal nodes have exactly 2 children).

solution.result: FALSE. A binary tree can have any number of leaves; it need not be a power of 2. Answer: B. FALSE

questions.formula_note: In a perfect binary tree of height h, the number of leaves is exactly 2^h. But general binary trees have no such restriction on leaf count.

questions.question:
Construct a binary tree whose preorder traversal is:
K L N M P Q S T
and inorder traversal is:
N L K P M S Q T

Draw the resulting binary tree and identify the root node.
questions.to_find: The binary tree (and its root) constructed from the given pre-order and in-order traversal sequences.

understand.plain: Pre-order gives us the root first. In-order splits the tree: everything to the left of the root in the in-order sequence is in the left subtree, everything to the right is in the right subtree. We apply this rule recursively to reconstruct the entire tree. The root of the whole tree is the first element of the pre-order sequence: K.

keywords.explain: Root is visited first, then left subtree (pre-order), then right subtree (pre-order). The first element is always the root.
keywords.example: Pre-order K L N M P Q S T means K is root.

keywords.explain: Left subtree is visited first, then root, then right subtree. Splitting by root position gives left and right subtrees.
keywords.example: In-order N L K P M S Q T: K is root, so {N,L} is left subtree, {P,M,S,Q,T} is right subtree.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='500' height='300' viewBox='0 0 500 300'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='500' height='300' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='250' y='26' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>Reconstructed Binary Tree</text><circle cx='250' cy='55' r='18' class='hi'/><text x='250' y='61' class='tag ink' text-anchor='middle'>K</text><circle cx='130' cy='110' r='18' class='hi'/><text x='130' y='116' class='tag ink' text-anchor='middle'>L</text><circle cx='340' cy='110' r='18' class='hi'/><text x='340' y='116' class='tag ink' text-anchor='middle'>M</text><circle cx='90' cy='165' r='18' class='ok'/><text x='90' y='171' class='tag' fill='white' text-anchor='middle'>N</text><circle cx='280' cy='165' r='18' class='hi'/><text x='280' y='171' class='tag ink' text-anchor='middle'>P</text><circle cx='400' cy='165' r='18' class='hi'/><text x='400' y='171' class='tag ink' text-anchor='middle'>Q</text><circle cx='370' cy='220' r='18' class='ok'/><text x='370' y='226' class='tag' fill='white' text-anchor='middle'>S</text><circle cx='430' cy='220' r='18' class='ok'/><text x='430' y='226' class='tag' fill='white' text-anchor='middle'>T</text><line x1='238' y1='68' x2='142' y2='97' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='262' y1='68' x2='328' y2='97' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='118' y1='123' x2='100' y2='151' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='328' y1='123' x2='292' y2='151' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='352' y1='123' x2='390' y2='151' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='392' y1='178' x2='378' y2='206' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='408' y1='178' x2='422' y2='206' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='250' y='285' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>Root = K; Leaves = N, P, S, T</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Binary tree with root K. Left subtree: L with left child N. Right subtree: M with left child P and right child Q; Q has left child S and right child T.

given.aim: Reconstruct the binary tree from its pre-order and in-order traversal sequences.

terms.term: Pre-order: K L N M P Q S T
terms.meaning: The root-left-right visiting sequence of the binary tree.
terms.example: First element K is the root; next comes the left subtree (L N), then right subtree (M P Q S T).
terms.connects: Tells us the root at each recursive step.

terms.term: In-order: N L K P M S Q T
terms.meaning: The left-root-right visiting sequence of the binary tree.
terms.example: K appears at position 3 (0-indexed: 2) in in-order, so left subtree has 2 nodes: {N, L}; right subtree has 5 nodes: {P, M, S, Q, T}.
terms.connects: Tells us which nodes are in the left vs right subtree at each step.

terms.meaning: Apply the rule 'first element of pre-order is root; split in-order by root to get left and right subtrees' recursively.
terms.example: After placing K as root, recursively reconstruct left subtree from pre=[L,N], in=[N,L] and right subtree from pre=[M,P,Q,S,T], in=[P,M,S,Q,T].
terms.connects: Uniquely determines the full tree structure at every level.

given.plan: Step through the reconstruction recursively: (1) first pre-order element = root, (2) find root in in-order to split into left and right subtrees, (3) repeat for each subtree using appropriate sub-sequences.

steps.title: Find root and split into left/right subtrees
steps.formula_raw: Root = pre-order[0]; Left inorder = inorder[0..pos-1]; Right inorder = inorder[pos+1..end]
steps.apply:
Pre-order: K L N M P Q S T -> root = K.
In-order: N L K P M S Q T -> K is at index 2.
Left subtree in-order: N L (2 nodes).
Right subtree in-order: P M S Q T (5 nodes).
Left subtree pre-order: L N (next 2 from pre-order after K).
Right subtree pre-order: M P Q S T (remaining 5).
steps.note: The number of nodes in the left subtree from in-order (2) tells us how many elements to take from pre-order for the left subtree.

steps.title: Reconstruct left subtree (pre=[L,N], in=[N,L])
steps.apply:
Root of left subtree = L (first of pre-order [L,N]).
In-order [N,L]: L is at index 1. Left of L = {N} (1 node), right of L = empty.
So L has left child N and no right child.
Left subtree rooted at L: L -> N (left child, leaf).
steps.note: N is a leaf node (appears alone in both remaining sub-sequences).

steps.title: Reconstruct right subtree (pre=[M,P,Q,S,T], in=[P,M,S,Q,T])
steps.apply:
Root = M (first of pre=[M,P,Q,S,T]).
In-order [P,M,S,Q,T]: M is at index 1. Left of M = {P} (1 node), right of M = {S,Q,T} (3 nodes).
Left subtree of M: pre=[P], in=[P] -> P is a leaf.
Right subtree of M: pre=[Q,S,T], in=[S,Q,T].
  Root = Q. In-order [S,Q,T]: Q at index 1. Left={S}, right={T}.
  So Q has left child S (leaf) and right child T (leaf).
Right subtree: M -> P (left, leaf), Q (right); Q -> S (left, leaf), T (right, leaf).
steps.note: Fully determined: M is the root of the right subtree with a 3-level structure under it.

steps.title: Assemble the complete tree
steps.apply:
Complete tree structure:
K (root)
  Left: L
    Left: N (leaf)
  Right: M
    Left: P (leaf)
    Right: Q
      Left: S (leaf)
      Right: T (leaf)
Verification:
  Pre-order of this tree: K, L, N, M, P, Q, S, T [CORRECT]
  In-order of this tree: N, L, K, P, M, S, Q, T [CORRECT]
steps.note: Both traversals match, confirming the reconstruction is correct.

solution.result: The root of the reconstructed binary tree is K. The full tree: K(root) -> L(left child) with N as L's left child; K -> M(right child) with P as M's left child and Q as M's right child; Q has left child S and right child T.

questions.formula_note: Binary tree reconstruction algorithm: given pre-order and in-order, the first pre-order element is the root; find it in in-order to partition left and right subtrees; recurse. This runs in O(n^2) naively or O(n) with hash-map.

questions.question: Define the height of a binary tree or subtree and also define a height-balanced (AVL) tree.
questions.answer: Height: the number of edges on the longest path from the root to a leaf (or equivalently, the number of levels minus 1). AVL tree: a binary search tree in which for every node, the heights of its left and right subtrees differ by at most 1 (balance factor in {-1, 0, +1}).
questions.to_find: Formal definitions of: (1) height of a binary tree, and (2) height-balanced (AVL) tree.

understand.plain: The height of a binary tree tells us the length of the longest root-to-leaf path. An AVL tree is a special BST where this height is kept balanced: at every single node, the left and right subtrees must have heights that differ by at most 1. This ensures the tree stays O(log n) in height, making search, insert, and delete efficient.

keywords.explain: The number of edges on the longest path from the root to any leaf node. An empty tree has height -1 (or 0 by some conventions); a single-node tree has height 0 (or 1).
keywords.example: A tree with root -> child -> grandchild has height 2 (two edges on the longest path).

keywords.explain: A BST where for every node, |height(left subtree) - height(right subtree)| <= 1. This balance factor is maintained after every insert/delete via rotations.
keywords.example: A BST where root has left subtree of height 2 and right subtree of height 3 is still AVL (difference = 1).

understand.visual_alt: An AVL tree with root 30, left subtree rooted at 20 (with children 10 and 25) and right subtree rooted at 40 (with right child 50). Balance factors: root=0, node 20=0, node 40=-1. All |bf|<=1 so it is AVL. Height=2.

given.aim: Provide formal definitions of binary tree height and AVL (height-balanced) tree.

terms.meaning: h(empty) = -1 (or 0); h(T) = 1 + max(h(left subtree), h(right subtree)) for non-empty T.
terms.example: Single node: h = 0. Root with one child: h = 1. Root with child and grandchild: h = 2.
terms.connects: Height determines tree performance: operations on a BST take O(h) time, so minimizing h is key.

terms.meaning: bf(v) = h(right subtree of v) - h(left subtree of v).
terms.example: If left height = 2 and right height = 1, bf = 1-2 = -1.
terms.connects: AVL property requires |bf(v)| <= 1 for every node v in the tree.

terms.meaning: After every insert or delete, if any node's |bf| becomes 2, perform rotations (single or double) to restore the AVL property.
terms.example: A right-right imbalance is fixed by a left rotation; a right-left imbalance requires a right rotation then a left rotation.
terms.connects: The invariant guarantees height O(log n), keeping all BST operations O(log n) worst-case.

given.plan: State the recursive definition of height. State the AVL balance condition. Mention the significance: AVL trees maintain O(log n) height, giving efficient operations.

steps.title: Definition of Height of a Binary Tree
steps.apply:
The height of a binary tree T is defined recursively:
- If T is empty (null), h(T) = -1.
- Otherwise, h(T) = 1 + max(h(T.left), h(T.right)).

Equivalently, the height equals the number of edges on the longest path from the root node to any leaf node.

Examples:
- Empty tree: height = -1.
- Single node (only root, no children): height = 0.
- Root with one child: height = 1.
- Root -> child -> grandchild: height = 2.
steps.note: Some textbooks define height as the number of nodes on the longest path (instead of edges), giving h(single node) = 1. Either convention is acceptable as long as it is consistently used.

steps.title: Definition of Height-Balanced (AVL) Tree
steps.apply:
An AVL tree (named after Adelson-Velsky and Landis, 1962) is a Binary Search Tree (BST) in which, for every node v:
  |h(v.left_subtree) - h(v.right_subtree)| <= 1

This condition is called the height-balance property or AVL property.
The quantity bf(v) = h(v.right) - h(v.left) is called the balance factor of node v.
For a valid AVL tree, bf(v) in {-1, 0, +1} for every node v.

When an insertion or deletion violates this property, tree rotations are performed:
- Single right rotation (LL case)
- Single left rotation (RR case)
- Double rotation: right then left (RL case)
- Double rotation: left then right (LR case)

Result: An AVL tree with n nodes has height Theta(log n), ensuring O(log n) time for search, insert, and delete.
steps.note: The height of an AVL tree is at most 1.44 * log_2(n+2) - 0.328, guaranteeing near-optimal performance.

solution.result: Height of a binary tree: h(empty)=-1; h(T)=1+max(h(left),h(right)) — the number of edges on the longest root-to-leaf path. AVL tree: a BST where for every node, the heights of left and right subtrees differ by at most 1 (|bf|<=1), maintained via rotations to guarantee O(log n) height.

questions.formula_note: AVL (Adelson-Velsky and Landis) tree: height-balanced BST with balance factor |bf| <= 1 at every node. Height invariant: h(AVL with n nodes) = O(log n). Rotations (single/double) restore the AVL property after insertions/deletions.
